turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: We'll hear argument first this morning in Case 14簫1280, Heffernan v. City of Paterson, New Jersey. Mr. Frost.

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Public employees have a right not to be demoted on patronage grounds. It does not matter if you are affiliated with a specific party or that you are nonaffiliated. It does not matter if you are mistakenly perceived by your employer or supervisor that you're engaged in political association to be protected by the First Amendment.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: How would you define the right at issue in this case?

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: The 簫簫 the issue here is 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: How would you define the right that your client wishes this Court to vindicate?

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: I'm defining the right in that pursuant to Elrod and its progeny, that there 簫簫 it is not necessary to have any affirmative acts, that by virtue of being a public employee, he has the right not to engage in political association.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, that's just a restatement of 簫簫 of Elrod. I 簫簫 I 簫簫 would 簫簫 would 簫簫 would it be fair to the proposition that you are putting before the Court to say that you're asserting the right to be free from government inquiry into an oversight of your views? Would that be a fair statement?

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: That would be a fair statement, Justice Kennedy.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Because 簫簫 look, do we 簫簫 it sounds to me from the way you began your argument that we take this case on the assumption that, if he had picked up the sign, that if he had been supporting the candidate for chief of police who was challenging the incumbent, if he had been engaged in the activity, that would be protected. He could not have been demoted. If 簫簫 you want us to take the case on that 簫簫 do we have to accept that proposition for you to prevail in this case?

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: No, you do not have to accept that proposition. Proposition is just clearly that, as a public employee, he has a right to either associate or nonassociate, and he doesn't have to commit an affirmative act in support, in this case, of the mayor's opponent, which was Chief Spagnola.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, but the 簫簫 the First Amendment talks about abridging freedom of speech, and I thought the case came to us on the proposition that he wasn't engaging in speech at all. That he was not engaging in association, he was not engaging in trying to convey a message, he was just picking up a sign for his mother. And if that's the basis on which the case comes to us, I'm not sure how he can say his freedom of speech has been abridged.

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: In this case, Mr. Chief Justice, the case comes to us with respect to association. With respect to speech, speech is governed by a different doctrine than association. There is no need to do a Pickering analysis in this case.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: He wasn't associating with anybody any more 簫簫 any more than he was speaking. He was doing neither one.

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: Justice Scalia 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: He was associate 簫簫 associating with his mother, I suppose, in picking up the sign for her. But he was not expressing any political view. He was not associating with a political party. What case of ours vindicates the right that 簫簫 that Justice Kennedy described to you and which you readily agreed with? What 簫簫 what case of ours vindicates that 簫簫

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: Elrod would stand for that proposition.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Elrod 簫簫

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: Elrod 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Elrod says you have a First Amendment right to favor a political party or not favor a political party, and you cannot be fired for doing so. That's not what happened here.

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: But he was not favoring a political party.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Exactly. He was not expressing any First Amendment view whatever. I mean, he was fired for the wrong reason, but there's no constitutional right not to be fired for the wrong reason. If he was fired because his 簫簫 his 簫簫 his 簫簫 his government employer thought he had committed a felony and he hadn't, he might 簫簫 he might have a cause of action under some statute. But there 簫簫 there 簫簫 there's no constitutional right not to be fired for the wrong reason, and that's what happened here.

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: There's a constitutional right, Justice Scalia, to be able to be free from patronage decisions and to be discharged or demoted on patronage grounds. And in Elrod 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Where do we say that? We never said that. Elrod and 簫簫 and Branti are 簫簫 are cases decided under the First Amendment. The First Amendment guarantees the right to freedom of speech and freedom of association. Your client was neither speaking nor associating. So how could he possibly have a cause of action under the First Amendment?

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: He doesn't need to speak, and he doesn't need to take a position. The Rutan plaintiffs, they didn't take a position with respect to promotion or transfers. They took 簫簫 the fact of the matter is, is 簫簫 is that since they were not affiliated with the Republican party or supporting the Republican party or endorsed by the Republican party, none of those individuals would have been promoted or transferred. They didn't take any affirmative acts.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: I don't understand 簫簫 I don't understand your answer. What 簫簫 what expressive activity did he engage in? He wasn't 簫簫 he was not allegedly demoted for failing to support the mayor. He was allegedly demoted for seemingly supporting the mayor's opponent.

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: In 簫簫 in Waters this Court looked at the motive of the employer, the motive of government. If government perceives that you are engaging in a political activity and the motive is to suppress one's beliefs and associations or nonassociations, then you look at it through government's analysis, and it's their basis of their facts that you are evaluating. Here they evaluated the facts that he was engaged in campaigning. Officer Heffernan went to a political gathering. He went and picked up a sign. What was misperceived by his employer was the fact of his intent. He said that, I'm not intending to support Spagnola, but he is doing all those incidents that are core First Amendment activities.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, let me just 簫簫 to clarify what I 簫簫 how I thought the case was presented. Let's say the employer comes in to Smith and says, you know, Smith, I saw you getting a 簫簫 a 簫簫 a political sign, and you're 簫簫 and you're 簫簫 you're fired. And Smith says, it wasn't me. I was off, you know, in a different town then. In other words, it's a pure mistake of fact. Your answers, it seems to me, you try to get advantage of the fact that there 簫簫 you could perhaps have argued this was expressive activity. Say, oh, he was at a meeting, a political event. He was getting a sign. But your theory, I thought, didn't depend on that at all.

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: It 簫簫 it 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: It was simply a mistake of fact. And then 簫簫 now, can the person who wasn't even there, can he bring a First Amendment challenge to his dismissal?

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: Yes, Mr. Chief Justice.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: And the point that I was making 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: And then again 簫簫 but it's still not clear to me: What is the right that he's asserting? And 簫簫 and I'll back up while you're thinking about that. Is 簫簫 can a local government say that all our employees must be neutral in campaigns and must not take part in campaigns?

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: Well 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: They can vote, but they can't take part in campaigns? Like the Hatch Act?

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: Well, there are Hatch Acts. There was no provision here.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Can the government insist on neutrality?

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: I think the government can have a policy after balancing the interests between that of the employer and that of the position.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Is that an issue 簫簫

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: I was not saying 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Is that an issue 簫簫 is that an issue in this case? Is there any allegation that there was such a policy of neutrality that no employee could engage in political activity?

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: Okay. Justice Ginsburg, there was no such policy in this case involving 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I'm sorry. I thought there was an unwritten policy.

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: There 簫簫 there was a policy that Chief Wittig said that existed with respect to members of his staff only. However, with respect to that policy, there's no testimony regarding that policy. In other words, the people that worked in the Chief's office 簫簫 there were four individuals who were officers 簫簫 none of them ever heard of that policy. Also, that policy was never raised below in any of the motions for summary judgment or the motion before the Third Circuit twice. So we believe that that motion is waived. But to answer your question, Your Honor, is the fact that no one knew of this policy. So 簫簫 so he could say he had a policy and he could make reference to it, but it's just not supported by any evidence in this case.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Is there a remedy for your client, apart from this action under the First Amendment? Does he have civil service protections of any kind or 簫簫 or 簫簫 I don't know 簫簫 collective bargaining?

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: Your Honor 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Let's just say: What would happen if the boss comes in and says, you know, for the 簫簫 you didn't turn off the lights last night. You're fired. Can he 簫簫 is there a way for him to say, I was on leave yesterday. It wasn't me.

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: Well, that's not a constitutional violation for 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: No, no. My point is that maybe this shouldn't be a constitutional violation if there are adequate remedies to address what may or may not be a First Amendment issue.

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, there are 簫簫 there were no other remedies.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: There is a 簫簫 State of New Jersey has a State Civil Rights Act which mimics 1983. Just because there is a 簫簫 such a right, doesn't mean that you can diminish the First Amendment and give up your First Amendment rights. So clearly, we proceeded with the fact that there was a violation of the First Amendment just because it was created by the State. There is no civil service 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: What 簫簫 what is the New Jersey Act? That's a strange act. It protects State employment only against First Amendment violations?

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: No, it 簫簫 it mimics 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I can't imagine that. It must 簫簫 it must have other protections for State employee 簫簫 employees.

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: With respect to the New Jersey statute, it's just identical to a 1983 cause of action, so it doesn't give you any additional rights. Officer Heffernan 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: How does it read? You have to violate the Federal constitution to get relief under New Jersey law?

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: Do you have to?

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Yeah. That's what 1983 says. What is 簫簫 what does New Jersey law say?

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: He proceeded in Federal court. He has every right to proceed in Federal court for a First Amendment violation.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: What does the New Jersey law say? You brought it up. I didn't bring it up. What does it say?

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: New Jersey law, the statute just mimics a 1983 cause of action.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: So it says 簫簫 it says if you violate 簫簫 you can't fire a State employee in violation of the Federal constitution. That's what the New Jersey law says. What a strange law.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: You don't have the text of it, do you?

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: I'm sorry.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: The text of the New Jersey law, so we can know what we're talking about.

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: Sure. I mean, this was 簫簫 New Jersey law can't create substantive rights. So with respect to this matter, he's also not protected by civil service, either.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, Mr. Goldstein's brief says New Jersey law provides a statutory right of action for an attempt to violate an individual's civil rights. Is he wrong?

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: An attempt to violate?

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: It 簫簫 it is included in there, but 簫簫 but that doesn't give you an exclusive right. You can't diminish his First Amendment rights.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: No. The question is not whether the New Jersey law somehow abrogates First Amendment rights. The question is whether he has a 簫簫 an independent remedy under State law. That's the question, I think, that's being asked.

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: He could have a remedy under State law. But it 簫簫 in this instance, he pursued it, his First Amendment rights.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: How could he? If we say there's no First Amendment right, what civil right has he 簫簫 was violated if 簫簫 if what Mr. Goldstein says is the 簫簫

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: The civil right 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: 簫簫 is the state of the law attempting to violate civil rights? What right, if we say there isn't the First Amendment?

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: The right not to associate.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Well, if we say it doesn't exist. You lose this case.

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: If what doesn't exist, Justice Sotomayor?

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Just that, the right you're claiming.

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: He has every right to bring this claim in Federal court as 簫簫

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: He may have broader substantive rights under New Jersey law. I would be somewhat surprised if that were not the case. The civil rights to which this 簫簫 the New Jersey statute refers may refer to rights under the New Jersey constitution or other New Jersey laws.

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: There are New Jersey laws. But 簫簫 but just because 簫簫 again, it doesn't abrogate your First Amendment rights. So the fact 簫簫 it shouldn't matter whether or not he 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Just 簫簫

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: 簫簫 engaged 簫簫 there could have been a violation of any type of New Jersey statute. It wasn't alleged in this case. It was never brought up by Respondents in this case during 簫簫 during any of the arguments in any of the briefs. And 簫簫 and therefore, with respect to this matter, it's our position that he has every right to maintain this litigation with respect to violation of his First Amendment right. And here, he is alleging that he has the right not to associate. And that right really stems from the fact that we have 簫簫 that this Court has considered in Elrod and its progeny. Furthermore, the Third Circuit makes its mistake in requiring Heffernan to actually engage in some type of political activity, campaigning. That's not necessary, as I've indicated, with respect to Elrod and the Rutan plaintiffs. Additionally, the Third Circuit's decision in this case is actually 簫簫 lacks common sense. I mean, if you take a hypothetical with two police officers going to pick up a sign, and when they go to pick up the sign they're at a campaign gathering, and one police officer states to his employer, yeah, I was supporting Spagnola, and Mr. Heffernan would say, no, I'm 簫簫 I'm not supporting it. I'm doing this to pick up a sign for my mother, should make no difference. The outcome is still the same in the sense that they're both engaged in that activity. The only difference is, is that the employer perceived Mr. Heffernan as engaging in protected activity. They went to stifle and squash his rights of association or nonassociation. Their motive was to suppress that. And clearly, that has a chilling effect on other employees. I mean, it's just very clear from the testimony in this case. And if you go to the Appendix, page 50, what is very telling about this case is the fact that when he went to pick up the sign, there was a councilman there who was a chairperson of the election. And he says to Heffernan, says, boy, you better be careful. Maybe you should come back later while we're 簫簫 because we're hanging up these signs. And that clearly shows the chilling effect that it would have not only in this police department or in the City of Paterson, but other employees in different jurisdictions and different areas would have the same issues. You would have to think twice before you did something. If you went to a political gathering or a campaign, or you went to hear a speaker speak and you picked up a pamphlet and put it on your desk, the 簫簫 if your employer saw that and they didn't like that candidate and they took action against you, you would see that that is action based on a motive to suppress one's rights.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: All of those things would be true if the mayor gave a speech saying I am going to fire anybody who's not a Republican. All of those things would be true. Would it chill people 簫簫 blah blah blah 簫簫 would there be a cause of action? Would anybody have a cause of action because of that speech?

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: Because of the speech itself?

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Yeah, just the speech. He hasn't fired anybody. He's just said I am going to fire anybody who's not a Republican, or anybody who's not a Republican will not get promoted.

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: Justice Scalia 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Does anybody have a cause of action for that?

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: There would be no claim, because no action was taken. So 簫簫 so part 簫簫 you have the perception that they engaged in an activity.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Exactly. So 簫簫 so what counts is whether action was taken for a particular reason, not whether you chilled 簫簫 whether you chilled people, which you're arguing to us is this is unconstitutional because it chills other people. That 簫簫 that doesn't 簫簫 that just doesn't carry water.

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: Justice Scalia, what carries water is the fact that in this instance, Heffernan was denoted on the employer's mistaken perception that he was engaged in activity, and you don't need to engage in that activity.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Would you 簫簫 would you say that he was demoted because he gave the appearance of exercising his First Amendment rights?

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: Yes. Because they perceived it that he was exercising his rights, and the fact that he actually was not engaged in any political activity should make no difference with respect to the motivation outcome of what took place with Mr. Heffernan. The issue was clearly that it was ill will. It was because it was against the administration, and they took that action to suppress that belief, and it chills other. Mr. Chief Justice, may I reserve the remaining time 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: You may.

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: 簫簫 if there are no other questions? Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Ms. Anders. FOR UNITED STATES, AS AMICUS CURIAE, SUPPORTING THE PETITIONER

turns.name: ANDERS
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: I'd like to start with Justice Kennedy's question about how we define the right here. We think that Petitioner has a First Amendment right not to have adverse action taken against him by his employer for the unconstitutional purpose of suppressing disfavored political beliefs. He was directly injured 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: And what's your best case for that proposition?

turns.name: ANDERS
turns.text: Well, we think that there are 簫簫 there are two aspects of this 簫簫 this Court's case law I think that support that, and then I'd like to get back to your concern about probing of beliefs. But we think that the way that the Court defined the right at issue in Branti and Rutan, and again in O'Hare, demonstrates that an employee doesn't have to affirmatively exercise his First Amendment rights first. So in those courts 簫簫 in 簫簫 in those cases the court said that the plaintiff can show a constitutional violation of the First Amendment simply by showing that the employer acted for the unconstitutional purpose of suppressing disfavored political 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: And those cases, Ms. Anders 簫簫 I missed what you said those cases were.

turns.name: ANDERS
turns.text: That was Branti 簫簫 this is at 445 U.S. at 517 簫簫 again in Rutan, and then in 簫簫 in O'Hare, which is the government contract 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: You say in all of those cases, no First Amendment right was being exercised, right?

turns.name: ANDERS
turns.text: No.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Is that your point?

turns.name: ANDERS
turns.text: Well, I think actually in the Rutan case there were 簫簫 there were three plaintiffs, and it wasn't clear, actually, whether they had engaged in any affirmative exercise of their First Amendment 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Was it clear that they hadn't?

turns.name: ANDERS
turns.text: So what the court said was that they had 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Was it clear that they hadn't?

turns.name: ANDERS
turns.text: It was not clear whether they had or hadn't, and the court didn't inquire into that. So what had happened there was that the 簫簫 the plaintiffs had been 簫簫 had adverse action taken against them because they had lacked the support of the correct Republican party officials. That's all the court says about their allegations. There are any number of reasons they could have lacked that support. They could have affirmatively refused, obviously, to seek the support, which would be an exercise of First Amendment rights. But on the other hand, they may simply have not had the time to seek the right support, or they may have been ignorant of the requirement in the first place. Those wouldn't have involved an affirmative exercise.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Yeah. I thought in all of these cases up to now, whenever anybody's fired, demoted, or even not promoted, it hurts and 簫簫 and you want to bring a lawsuit sometimes. Up to now, those lawsuits would have to show I was asserting a First Amendment right, and it's for that reason that I was not promoted. But what you're saying is, you don't have to show that at all. All you have to assert is that the reason I was not promoted was that the 簫簫 the employer believed that I was thus and so or not thus and so. You don't have to show any assertion of a First Amendment right. You just show that the 簫簫 the employer liked Republicans, and that's enough.

turns.name: ANDERS
turns.text: Well, I think in the employment context, I think there's no question that the plaintiff was directly injured by the unconstitutional condition. But to respond directly to your point, I think 簫簫 I don't think there's a serious concern here that there will be a meritless 簫簫 a flood of meritless lawsuits or anything like that, and that's primarily for two reasons. I take your point that 簫簫 that, you know, this will expand the universe of 簫簫 of litigation somewhat, but we already know in the statutory context, the courts have recognized suits based on a mistaken perception, and 簫簫 and in that context we haven't seen any flood of meritless lawsuits. And with respect to Elrod claims specifically, I think it's always been fairly easy for plaintiffs to allege that a particular affiliation of belief, I was in the Republican party 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: How do you know we haven't seen a flood of meritless lawsuits in that context?

turns.name: ANDERS
turns.text: Well, we 簫簫 we haven't in the statutory context.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Yeah, the one that you were just referring to.

turns.name: ANDERS
turns.text: Well, we looked at this, it 簫簫 it doesn't come up very much. And when it does, it 簫簫 it looks to us that 簫簫 that courts have been able to use the standard technique to 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: You're looking at the reported decisions rather than 簫簫 you haven't done any survey to see how many complaints have been filed in 簫簫 in this type of case?

turns.name: ANDERS
turns.text: No. But when you looked to reported decisions, what you see is that courts are able to use Twombly and Iqbal and summary judgment in order to get rid of claims where the allegations aren't plausible or there's no evidence. But I also think if the plaintiffs are going to have to allege that 簫簫 that they actually held the beliefs in question, this is going to raise exactly the concern that Justice Kennedy mentioned earlier about oversight of beliefs, probing into beliefs. So this will affect all Elrod cases. So every time an Elrod case is brought, the defendant will have the incentive to say 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: So are you saying there's a right to be secure from government oversight of your beliefs?

turns.name: ANDERS
turns.text: Well, I think the Court recognized in O'Hare that that is a particular concern in the affiliation context. We 簫簫 we don't want courts to have to 簫簫 to examine the nature and extent of the plaintiff's beliefs or associations.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I mean, that's a good idea, maybe. And maybe 簫簫 maybe it should be in some civil service act, but where do you find it in the First Amendment?

turns.name: ANDERS
turns.text: Well, I think the right 簫簫 the right in question is the right not to be subject to a test of political affiliation 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Is 簫簫 is that because you're concerned with a chill, or is there some other right that is 簫簫 is somehow affected that's a First Amendment right of the individual? You turn around and you say the government cannot act for an unconstitutional purpose, but we usually ask how is the defendant hurt? What is his right? That's still a little unclear to me.

turns.name: ANDERS
turns.text: We think the defendant has been hurt in the constitutional sense because the way that the court has defined the right in question is the right not to be subject to a test of political affiliation when it's not a reasonable job requirement.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: I don't know why 簫簫

turns.name: ANDERS
turns.text: And I do think what the Court said in 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Please.

turns.name: ANDERS
turns.text: What the Court said in O'Hare, essentially, was that 簫簫 we're not necessarily talking about a separate right not to be subject to probing of beliefs, but I think the Court was suggesting that we shouldn't have First Amendment tests, if we can help it, that lead the Court to have to probe into a plaintiff's beliefs. And that is exactly what will happen.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: I don't know why the right isn't the right to be free from arbitrary employment action based on a mistake. That's his objection here. You made a mistake; you thought I was, you know, being politically active. I wasn't. I mean, isn't 簫簫 is there such a right under New Jersey law, you can't be fired for an arbitrary reason, or you can't be fired for a mistaken reason?

turns.name: ANDERS
turns.text: I think with, at least with respect to some job actions, there 簫簫 there would be such a right under New Jersey law, and that's 簫簫 I'm not sure whether it would apply here, but I think the gravamen of 簫簫 of plaintiff's complaint here is not that he was fired for an inaccurate reason; it's that he was 簫簫 he was demoted because 簫簫 for an improper purpose, the perception that he was engaging in protected First Amendment activity. And 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: You've brought up in your brief, I think, that 簫簫 that the employer might have had a contract簫type policy. I think you said we should remand to determine whether this employer said nobody engages in political activity.

turns.name: ANDERS
turns.text: Well, I think 簫簫 I think if you rule for the Petitioner here, there would be a remand anyway because the Court hasn't 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Is it based on the facts up till now to think that there was such a policy?

turns.name: ANDERS
turns.text: Well, so there 簫簫 there is, I think, a factual dispute about this. So if you look at the 簫簫 the summary judgment filings, this is document No. 189 on Pacer, there 簫簫 there are, in 簫簫 in the Respondents' statement of undisputed facts, some assertions that this was in fact a neutrality policy. Now you definitely have a dispute about that coming back from the other side. Petitioner says that nobody knew about that policy. So we think that is one thing that potentially the lower courts would have to look at, decide whether it was preserved and 簫簫 and resolve the dispute in that sense. But I do want to get back to another concern that I think Respondents' position raises. You know, we do think that 簫簫 that Petitioner has his own First Amendment right here. That was violated. We do think that when an employer acts against an employee based on a mistaken perception of his beliefs, that creates exactly the same chilling effect with which the Elrod cases are concerned. So the other employees will know that the employer expects political orthodoxy and will be chilled in their 簫簫 in their own association as a result. And I think there's another concern here that deepens the chilling effect, and that is that the logic of Respondents' position applies not only in cases of honest mistake, it also applies in cases in which the employer acts to exploit a loophole, essentially. You can imagine a situation in which the employer knows that some people have engaged in political affiliation. The employer wants to send a message, and it does that by acting against the employee it thinks didn't actually engage in that association. And so I think that is one of the dangers of Respondents' rule here, that it will create a loophole. And so what we are saying, essentially, is that it's just a narrow corollary to the Elrod right that the Court has already recognized. When the employer acts with the exact same intent that 簫簫 that is already impermissible under Elrod, and it injures the employee in his employment as a result, then the employer should be equally liable. It shouldn't get a free pass simply because it 簫簫 it's ill簫motivated and wrong. If there are no further questions.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Goldstein.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The doctrinal fight in the briefing is over whether the plaintiff in a case like this has to assert a constitutional right. And I think the questions so far have indicated an understanding that the plaintiff would have to. And the real question, then, is, is there actually a constitutional right here, one that we might define in other terms, including the one that Justice Kennedy identified as the right not to have your political views inquired in. And this is actually discussed in the Court's opinion in O'Hare in describing Elrod and Branti. And so I want to start there because we have to recognize that this right has never been recognized in any other political association case whatsoever. And there's no logical reason why it would occur specifically in the public employment context, which is an unconstitutional 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Can you explain, Mr. Goldstein, what sense it would make to say there are two people, the example Mr. Frost gave. One of them is a big supporter of Spagnola, and he gets demoted. And the other is politically neutral, and he gets demoted because the employer thinks he's a supporter of the rival mayoral candidate. What 簫簫 how could you make sense to a person of ordinary reason that one of them, the one who was in fact engaging in political activity, can't be demoted but the other one, who just was innocent, didn't do anything?

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: I'm sorry. I have to focus on, really, the precise wording of your hypothetical, but I think you may actually have something slightly different in mind, because you identify this, the second employee, one who is politically neutral. And the Court has said, in cases like Elrod and Branti, that the decision to remain neutral 簫簫 that is, I see this candidate, I see that candidate. I'm going to just not choose between them, because I 簫簫 you know, for any individual reason, that that's protected. That's a political choice. And in your precise hypothetical, both of those employees would have a claim. This case is different, and critically different. The other side has quite consciously, throughout the case, made only two arguments. The first is that he was a supporter of Spagnola. And he's given up on that. And the second is that he had no association whatsoever. He didn't have any more association than I did. He was politically oblivious. And so, Justice Ginsburg, if you were to ask me that question, which is what difference 簫簫 what 簫簫 what sense does it make? The sense that it makes is one is exercising a constitutional right, and one doesn't 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: So just to make sure I understand what you're saying, suppose there's somebody who comes into office, and it's a Democrat. And he says I want as many Democrats as possible in my office, no matter what jobs they're doing. Now, what you're saying is he can't demote or fire Republicans. He can't remove, demote, or fire people who have other political views, neither Democrat or Republican. But what he can do is he can get rid of anybody who is just politically apathetic. Is that your view?

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: If that was actually the policy, then technically, the answer to your question is yes under the First Amendment. And I'd like to explain why. I know it sounds anomalous. The reason is that those people 簫簫 there is nothing in the First Amendment that says that the government can't encourage people to be politically active. And that is entirely the point of Elrod and Branti.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: So you 簫簫 you encourage the person to be politically active and then fire him or her because they're politically active the wrong way?

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: No, sir.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Suppose the employee says, you know, I don't like the evening news. I 簫簫 I 簫簫 I like fiction. I 簫簫 I don't 簫簫 I don't know if I'm Republican or Democrat. I don't care.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: He 簫簫 he 簫簫 he cannot be fired? What is his right?

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: My point, Justice Kennedy, is that he may have a State law right; he does have a collective bargaining agreement right, but he doesn't have a First Amendment right, because he's not engaging in First Amendment簫protected activity. I do 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: See, I 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: The 簫簫 the government has 簫簫 excuse me.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: Sorry.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: The 簫簫 the 簫簫 the government has a right to compel him to declare one way or the other?

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: No 簫簫 Justice Kennedy, and that 簫簫 that brings us back to your question. And that is does he have a right not to have an inquiry into his political views. And that is, of course, not a right that's been recognized in any other political association context. When the Court discussed it in O'Hare, it did it in a very specific and really important way, and that is it looked at cases like Elrod and Branti, which are general policies. And what the Court has said there, is that when it's a sweeping policy, it's not necessary into 簫簫 to inquire into individual political beliefs. And what those cases ought to be understood as is applications of First Amendment overbreadth doctrine. It is commonplace in First Amendment law that if you have a general rule, and the general rule will be unconstitutional as applied to some people, and the other people involved weren't asserting First Amendment rights, the policy can be facially unconstitutional, and we don't inquire into the individual standing of the plaintiff. And that's what happens in Elrod and Branti. It's not necessary to inquire into the 簫簫 each individual employee. It is an entirely different 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: See, I had always thought that the 簫簫 the idea behind those cases is a different one. That the idea has to do with why the government acted. And once we say that the government acted for an impermissible purpose, which is to 簫簫 let's say in my hypothetical, get as many Democrats as possible into the government, once we say that's an impermissible purpose, it matters not at all whether the person is a Republican, an 簫簫 an independent, or somebody who has never thought about politics in his life, because the government is acting in a way that's wrongful, irrespective of that.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: And we just disagree. It's called an individual right, not a government wrong. The individual has to be engaging in 簫簫 whether it's expression or association, I actually thinks it's not contested, Justice Kagan, any more on the free speech side, that with cases like Waters, an employee cannot bring a First Amendment free speech claim that says, you know, I didn't actually engage in speech, but my employer thought I did. There is no First Amendment right not to have this individual inquiry. And Justice Kennedy, remember that our position, in particular 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: So does that mean that the government can compel speech of a person if the person really just doesn't care one way or the other?

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: No. It's a very important distinction. So let me give a hypothetical that ought to be hard for us, and that is Heffernan is asked by the chief of police, you know, do you support the mayor. He's completely agnostic, and he refuses to support the mayor, and he's transferred. The decision not to support or to be subjected to your hypothetical is a political choice. This case was framed by the plaintiff in a very specific way on purpose, and that is he disclaimed any such influence, any such pressure, any such choice that he was having to make. Now, the important piece about 簫簫 on this question of inquiry is that our position only applies to a party that doesn't claim anything other than being politically apathetic. So Justice Kennedy, there is no inquiry. Justice Kagan is quite right that if you have somebody who is an Independent or a Democrat or Republican, the First Amendment doesn't care. But if the plaintiff is going to pursue a claim that says I'm not engaged in association or speech, there is nothing to inquire into 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Mr. Goldstein, let's take a Title VII case, and the employer fires a woman because he thinks she's pregnant. She brings a sex discrimination case and alleges, well, I wasn't pregnant. I just was gaining weight. So she has no sex discrimination claim, then, because she wasn't pregnant?

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: Justice Ginsburg, the courts are divided. The position of the EEOC is that she would have a claim. I just think it's a good point for us that Congress can write laws that recognize such, regarded as claims.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Those 簫簫 those statutes focus on the employer. The employer cannot discriminate on the basis 簫簫

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: 簫簫 of sex.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: And that employer was doing that. The First Amendment does not focus on the government; it focuses on the citizen. The citizen has a right to free speech and free association.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: So 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: That's the difference between the two cases.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: Well, it will not surprise you that I agree, Justice Scalia. The 簫簫 Justice Kagan, if 簫簫 if we could just take this outside the public employment context. And the reason I want to do that is that we ought to be able to agree that the First Amendment rights aren't greater association of rights there. The Court has pointed out that there is a greater Federalism interest in managing the public employment workforce, and also that this is an unconstitutional conditions case. But just imagine the following simple 簫簫 you know, hypotheticals that relate just to this case. Imagine that the chief of police, with the same motivation, went up to Heffernan as he went to pick up the sign, grabbed the sign, and tore it up. Or imagine that Heffernan was trying 簫簫 was stopped from entering a parade in favor of Spagnola, but what he was actually trying to do was just cross the street. Or he went to the building where it is that Spagnola had the 簫簫 his headquarters, and the chief of police stopped him from going in, but he was actually going to his lawyer's office. Those are all the exact same motivation. And there 簫簫 I don't think there's any way the Court would recognize such a claim. It's a very sympathetic claim. Okay? I get the fact that we are very concerned that public employees not be transferred or demoted, but we have other laws and other regimes that are 簫簫 that fill that gap.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: What is 簫簫 what is the other law here? What 簫簫 what relief does he have?

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: He has two forms of relief. The first is the collective bargaining agreement. I will tell you that it is not in the record, but it is judicially noticeable. It is a public document. It's available on the government Web sites of the State of New Jersey, and it is exactly what you would expect. In fact, it's a little bit broader. It says that if you are 簫簫 you have an employment action that is inequitable, it is 簫簫 you 簫簫 it is grievable. The second thing is the attempt provision, which was discussed, and Justice Alito is right, that there are broader civil rights under New Jersey law. And this just makes sense. The Court, in cases 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, do 簫簫 do you agree that the Petitioner is entitled to relief under that provision of the collective bargaining agreement?

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: If 簫簫 if he 簫簫 his allegations of the facts are correct, yes.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: In your hypothetical about stopping the person from crossing the street, or, they think, entering the parade, the individual is 簫簫 has no right to insist that the government doesn't make a First Amendment judgment about his activities?

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: That's correct. Justice Kennedy 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: In 簫簫 in other words, the 簫簫 the 簫簫 the individual in the 簫簫 in 簫簫 in 簫簫 in 簫簫 the citizen of the United States, have no right, have 簫簫 have 簫簫 have no 簫簫 have no injury, have no interest in making sure the government doesn't evaluate everything they do from a political standpoint?

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: I think they have an interest, Justice Kennedy. I'm not saying that. We are concerned that people be able to have conscience, that they be able to make their own personal, private judgments. But what we're talking about here in the context in which there is a real concern that I would like to talk about, about whether it will interfere with the management of local government, that it's an affirmative constitutional right, it's a violation of the First Amendment. Now the reason you ought to be concerned, Justice Kennedy, is there is another side of the coin. Take it from the perspective of the supervisor. If this right is recognized, which is to say the plaintiff need not have engaged in any association, then the supervisor's expression of political views may well be chilled because he has to worry that any employee can say, look, I was regarded as politically active. If I could just give you this case again, and that is, take the sign out of it. The plaintiff says he was well known as a Spagnola friend and supporter. Okay? Imagine that that's discussed in the chief of police's office, but the chief of police thinks that 簫簫 that Heffernan really should be transferred. Okay? The chief 簫簫 if the Petitioner is right, he really has to worry because if it's discussed, then there's every reason that Heffernan can just bring a lawsuit saying, look, I wasn't actually involved in the campaign, but you did it because he was my friend. And that is a very significant consequence for the individual rights. Now, if we didn't have other protections that get to the concern about conscience, I could see the case being even stronger.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: We know that you can't 簫簫 let's assume in this particular position you can't be fired because you're a Republican or a Democrat and that's what they 簫簫 that's what 簫簫 what they do. But that 簫簫 the person did not engage in that activity. He's still 簫簫 he still is in the position of the government ascribing to him a political belief that he does not have.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: Justice Kennedy, that's right. The government thinks a lot of things about me, okay? Some of them are not very nice, I imagine, but 簫簫 and some are about my politics and that sort of thing. But there is not a constitutional right to have the government not think something about you. Just remember as well 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Here, they thought and they acted.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: Okay. Or to act, Justice Kennedy.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: It's not just something.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: Oh.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: It's 簫簫 the government is taking action against a person because the government thinks that that person is exercising First Amendment rights.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: Just 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: And I thought 簫簫 and unlike Justice Scalia 簫簫 that the thrust of the First Amendment is operating on government. It says government, thou shalt not 簫簫 thou shalt not act on the basis of someone's expression, speech or belief.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: Well, essentially all of the rights, individual rights in the Constitution, other than the antislavery provision, requires State action. They all talk about what the government can't do. But the government 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Yes, so here, the government acted. No question they demoted the person. This was a detective, and they put him back on the beat. So the government acted. Why did they act? Because they thought that this person was engaging in political activity.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: Well, Justice Ginsburg, let me just say that I don't think it's 簫簫 it's contested after the Petitioner's reply brief. You described this in First Amendment terms, that if this was a speech case, which it used to be, rather than an association case, he would lose. It is well settled in this Court's precedents that the threshold inquiry under Pickering is did the individual engage in the constitutionally protected activity? This actually is at issue, I should say, in the Court's other associational case, public employment association case, this sitting, where there is a significant issue of is there a different rule that applies 簫簫 when we're talking about a policy, Justice Kennedy 簫簫 that applies broadly to a lot of employees versus an individual, one簫off employment action which is what is at issue here? We think that's a critically important distinction, whether you look at this as kind of a Pickering standard case, where the first thing that has to happen is that the individual has to assert at least that they engaged in the constitutionally protected activity. If you have an Elrod and Branti簫type case, which is what's discussed in that part of O'Hare, it's an entirely different kettle of fish because there, you do have a general policy. You could see people being chilled. You could see the government taking a broad view of its employees.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: I guess I'm not sure how that works. It just seems to break down very easily, if you can't have a broad rule that says that you can fire everybody but Democrats. But you're saying that you are going to allow somebody to come in and fire people one at a time.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: Justice Kagan, that's, of course, not what I'm saying. What I'm saying is that under First Amendment Overbreadth Doctrine, when you have a general policy 簫簫 and this is what we think happened in Elrod and Branti 簫簫 the fact that you don't 簫簫 you do not inquire into the individual person's political views because the policy is facially unconstitutional. But it has never been the case in any context. And the other side has had every opportunity in the world. We cannot find any First Amendment case that says, you know what? You don't have to engage in constitutionally protected activity so long as the government thinks you did. And it's really a problem if that's the rule because it is the threshold thing that stops plaintiffs with meritless cases from getting out of the box. In all of these cases, if the plaintiff no longer has to say, I engaged in 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But you're saying 簫簫 and 簫簫 and I think you said this straight out 簫簫 you're saying I can come into an office 簫簫 I'm a Democrat. I can 簫簫 I can identify every person without a well簫known political view, every couch potato out there, just fire one after another, after another, after another. Replace them all with Democrats, change the entire character of the office, do it for a reason that I prefer one political view to any other, and that that will not be a violation of the First Amendment.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: That will 簫簫 two things. First, I think it is practically impossible because you would have to somehow magically pluck out the people who are politically apathetic from those that are politically neutral. I just don't think you can do it. But the second is that's right. The Constitution doesn't fix everything.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: You want this Court to hold that the government of the United States has a right to ascribe to a citizen views that he or she does not hold.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: Justice Kennedy, I think that that is not a First Amendment violation. I don't think the other side thinks it's a First Amendment violation. Remember that there's the materiality requirement.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: See, I had always thought that the First Amendment running through all our cases is an extremely strong strand. That part of the reason we have these protections is because we worry that government is doing things for impermissible reasons. That the government wants to create a world of speech in which everybody agrees with it and nobody opposes it. And that's a fundamental tenet of what the First Amendment and all our cases are about. And you're saying, oh, no. The government's motive doesn't really matter as long as you can't point to somebody who is holding up a sign.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: Justice Kagan, I think you are right. It is an important thread of this Court's decisions. And the Court has said that it is a necessary but not sufficient condition. Waters makes very clear, which is a First Amendment public employee case, that the individual has to have engaged in the constitutionally protected activity. Every one of 簫簫 Garcetti says the same thing. You have to have an individual 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Can I just approach the same thing from a different perspective? Suppose can Congress pass a law or a legislature pass a law that attempts to abridge the freedom of speech. Is that a violation of the First Amendment? It's an attempt.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: What would the attempt 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Well, let's say there doesn't have to be a thousand examples.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: The Court 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: You say that's the law. No one can espouse in a public place the political philosophy of Ruritanianism, okay?

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: No.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Okay. I mean, I doubt that they'll pass such a law 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: 簫簫 but I just 簫簫 substitute any one that you want for that. They pass it. And by the way, the six people who hold that philosophy all leave on a boat before the effective date, but 簫簫 or what's more likely, they bring a declaratory judgment action, and the law never takes effect. And therefore, it had no impact. And that happens every day of the week. I'm just wondering if such a law, which is an attempt 簫簫 it's right on the books as blatant as you want 簫簫 whether that violates the First Amendment. That's a serious question. I'm not taking a point of view. I want to know what you think.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: Justice Breyer, the reason that we allow 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Well, I just want to know, is it yes or no? Does it 簫簫 in your opinion, does it attempt to 簫簫 a clear attempt to violate the First Amendment in a statute? It has general application. Does it violate the First Amendment? Does it abridge the freedom of speech?

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: If it's only going to be an attempt and it's not going to succeed, no. The statute you describe is unconstitutional.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: It is unconstitutional.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: That's right. There are all kinds of times 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: It will have a lot of bad effects. It will have all kinds of chilling effects all over the place.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: If it's defined, Justice Breyer, as not going to succeed, if the statute doesn't say 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: No, no, by chance it happens not to succeed.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: Oh, no, Justice Breyer. If the person 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: It's my hypothetical.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: I know. I'm just 簫簫 I'm just trying to keep up with it. The 簫簫 in 簫簫 in the one that you just described, Justice Breyer, if it is the case that the law is going to go into effect, we do allow, including under First Amendment overbreadth grounds, an effort to bring a declaratory judgment action.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Of course. And I'm just asking you if, in fact, they think it will succeed, they want it to succeed, that's why they passed it, and through a fluke it fails, does the fact that it fails mean that it doesn't violate, it doesn't 簫簫 does it not 簫簫 does it or does it not violate the First Amendment?

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: It doesn't.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: It does not.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: And here would be an example.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: And he fails 簫簫 if it fails 簫簫

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: If I could just finish, Justice Breyer, just so 簫簫 I really do want to help. If Congress at the same time passed a law that said no Federal funds shall be used to implement the ban on talking about the political views of Ruritania, that is to say the law won't go into effect 簫簫 we tried, but the money was taken away from us 簫簫 it's not unconstitutional.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Or 簫簫 or you could say that the law is passed by Congress but vetoed by the President, is there a violation of the Constitution?

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: There would not be. But in my hypothetical vote in which 簫簫 or Justice Breyer's where it actually goes into effect 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: We're off on a tangent because there's no injury in this situation when the law doesn't go into effect. But coming back to what Justice Breyer, I believe, is attempting to say 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I don't know, Mr. Goldstein, that I understand either. And I think Justice Kagan asked you this. Why does it matter 簫簫 and we don't care whether someone is a Republican or not in Elrod and Branti and those cases 簫簫 and you say it's only because it's a policy. The intent of the government is to say I'm not going to promote anybody who is not a Democrat or not a Republican.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: More than a policy, it will actually have adverse consequences for someone trying to exercise their constitutional rights.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Well, someone, they have come along and not promoted people.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right? So you have to have someone come in and say I'm not a Democrat?

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: Justice 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I'm not a 簫簫

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: Sorry.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: 簫簫 Republican? Or you need someone to tell you what they are?

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: No. No. Common sense does not leave the courthouse, and that is if I have a policy that says, I will not hire Democrats, I think a court would understand that there are going to be Democrats who would apply for jobs. And there doesn't need to be an inquiry. But the 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So why isn't it simple to say, I'm not hiring you, or, I'm demoting you because you politically associated? And doesn't that chill the person from even walking by a campaign? Doesn't it chill others who do want to associate marginally?

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: No. It's a real 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I'm assuming there's no policy in place, or that there's not 簫簫

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: Justice 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: There's nothing to prevent this otherwise, like the Hatch Act.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: I think it's a really important point on the question of chilling because Elrod and Branti and the rules that also 簫簫 and O'Hare, which is a one簫off case, do say that you can't do this for political purposes if the person is actually exercising a constitutional right. So that we all agree that if the plaintiff here was a supporter of Spagnola, or even of the mayor, or had decided to remain politically neutral, this is a bizarre case that comes to you on the assumption that he is completely politically apathetic.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: It 簫簫 it is bizarre. And do 簫簫 do you really believe, Mr. Goldstein, that the Constitution does not solve all problems? You 簫簫 you 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: 簫簫 made a statement to that effect. You really believe that?

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: No, but I 簫簫 I 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: It doesn't solve every problem.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: No. But I do think that there is a concern that comes into play. And so I do not mean to demean the concern about the government having a sense of what individuals' political views are. But I'm saying that that happens, Your Honors, in all kinds of cases, whether it's redistricting, whether it's campaign finance. There's lots that we do to ascribe political views to people in this country. And adopting that doctrine is going to have pretty widespread consequences when it's not necessary. I do think that it should be common ground that there are multiple layers of protections for these employees. The basic rule 簫簫 and Justice Kennedy, remember, under the basic rule, the plaintiff has always come in and said, I'm a Democrat, right? The Elrod Branti rule when it's involved, one簫off cases. The opinion in O'Hare describes the political views of the tow truck company there. And it has never been regarded as a First Amendment problem when you don't actually exercise any First Amendment rights.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: But that is a First Amendment problem for the reason that lots of other people will have their speech chilled. And normally in the law there is a doctrine where the person who does the bad thing makes a mistake, he's held anyway. That's true of transferred intent. You shoot A, but you meant to shoot B. It's true of 簫簫 of attempts generally.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: It's not true of the Constitution.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Why not?

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: Well 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: That is to say I would think that a statute that has a chilling effect on the speech of millions of people but is directly aimed at A, B, and C, if because of some fluke A, B, and C are not themselves injured, nonetheless everybody else is, and it would violent the First Amendment.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: Under 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: That, I think, is what you have here, which is why I raised my point.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: Great. Justice Breyer 簫簫 and if you would just contrast in your own mind the statute that affects millions of people versus the beat cop who talks to one person in terms of its chilling effect, because First Amendment overbreadth doctrine was born because of your hypothetical. The concern that a broad policy or statute will have widespread effects. That is not anything like this. We have a guide 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Do you know of any case in which we have relied on chilling effects where what was at issue was a one簫off like this one as opposed to a 簫簫 a general policy which had a chilling effect? I don't know of any case.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: To the contrary, I can tell you that in 簫簫 in both Waters and Garcetti v. Ceballos, the Court said, look, we recognize the rule that's being proposed to us. So in Waters it was the idea that the public 簫簫 the employer's views wouldn't matter; it would just be whether the speech was protected. In Garcetti it was the question of whether it was the public employer's speech. And the public employee's argument in both those cases is that, look, these rules are going to chill speech because they are of uncertain boundaries. We need to have wide簫ranging expression. If I have to bring my case, there will be inquiries into my speech views or whatever, and the Court said we just have to balance things here. There is a real concern that is rooted in a history of the United States involving political patronage. We have 簫簫 the Court has never tried to extinguish politics from local government. And if you try to do that in New Jersey, we are going to be here a lot.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: And the 簫簫

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: And the question in this case seems to me to be highly artificial. It's sort of 簫簫 it's like a law school hypothetical.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: I agree.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: How often will it be the case that an employee will be unable to allege any expression or any association that is protected by the 簫簫 the First Amendment? It seems to me quite rare. And it may be that this case comes to us the way it does because the plaintiff was dealing with two things, one was the First Amendment, and the other 簫簫 I mean one was the issue that's 簫簫 the question of his motivation, and the other was this alleged policy prohibiting any kind of political activity. Even in the 簫簫 the person who is just apathetic, is there not a 簫簫 a First Amendment right to be 簫簫 say, I don't 簫簫 I don't like politics, I don't want anything to do with politics, I'm not going to register, I'm not going to vote?

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: All right. So Justice Alito, the Third Circuit has a rule discussed in the brief 簫簫 brief in opposition adopted in a case called Ghaly that says you do have the right to be politically apathetic. The reason the case is so bizarre is that the other side, for its own reasons, decided not to assert that right. Now, I will say that it does nonetheless matter because there are going to be other kinds of cases where you have a public employee that just is not asserting any rights at all, and is not involved, say, in environmentalism or gun rights or whatever. And the public employer, if it's thought just to have perceived the employee as having been involved in some association, then is subject to a claim. And our real 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I'm sorry. You have to 簫簫 you have to show some facts to draw that inference.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: Well 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I mean, you just can't say, I'm involved in this and the employer fired me because of that.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: Well, Justice Sotomayor 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: To show some connection between the firing and 簫簫

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: 簫簫 the political belief.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: I would just take you back to my hypothetical of getting rid of the sign. And his political view 簫簫 his support of Spagnola is discussed in 簫簫 in the chief's office, right? And then he has to be reassigned. And the chief has to really worry that he's going to be sued. The 簫簫 my point is this: The set of cases that you have to be concerned with, there have been no other cases we've been able to identify like this one. It's very small.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well 簫簫

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: But the downside risk is significant.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Mr. Goldstein, I mean, if somebody had come in to me before today's argument and just said, does the First Amendment prevent the government from punishing a person because that person does not share the government's views, I would have said, why, yes, of course the First Amendment protects that. That's the whole point of the First Amendment. And now you're telling me, no, the First Amendment does not prevent the government from punishing a person because that person doesn't share the government's views, unless that person is actively opposed to the government's views. But if that person just really could not care less, which a lot of people in this country could not care less; they don't vote, they don't pay attention, they wouldn't know who was running. But the government can punish that person because that person doesn't share the government's views. And I would have said, that is one strange doctrine.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: It 簫簫 it may be that I have not persuaded you in this case.

turns.name: GOLDSTEIN
turns.text: The 簫簫 the 簫簫 I will say, Justice Kagan, what you ask is, can the government do it? The government cannot because there are lots of other protections. And remember, if the person is politically neutral, it is the case that 簫簫 the right of political association is the right of political association. If you aren't engaging it, you aren't actively pursuing the right in any way, or even if you aren't active about it.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Frost, you have a minute left.

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice. A couple points that I want to make with respect to declaring one as a neutral. If Heffernan was engaged in political activity and said, I'm supporting Spagnola, of course he would be protected by the First Amendment. What I'm hearing is that if he said, I'm neutral, he would be covered; he would be protected. I see little difference between being a neutral and being agnostic in the sense that I'm not taking a position. So 簫簫 so 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I'm 簫簫 I'm so totally confused. I know it's the way it was presented to us, but I thought he testified that he had made a choice not to get involved in the campaign but that Spagnola was his friend and he supported him. So he wasn't neutral; he just wasn't engaging in associational conduct by choice. That's what I thought.

turns.name: FROST
turns.text: That's correct, Justice Sotomayor. And the point that I was trying to make was the fact that in this case he was not going to be exercising his right to vote or campaign for him, but 簫簫 but clearly he engaged in what we would consider core First Amendment activity by picking up that sign. The mistake that the employer made was actually one that they perceived him as actually campaigning on behalf of Spagnola. And that should make no difference for the simple reason that with respect to that activity, it's because the Court sees that the government is acting for an impermissible purpose, and that was the press 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.
